insights: All five agents have exactly 100 assignments each, indicating perfect workload balance with no agent experiencing disproportionate or excessive load.

No agent shows a sustained upward trend in monthly assignment counts—temporal variation exists but resolves into identical cumulative totals, refuting the hypothesis of increasing assignments or emerging overload.

The absence of any statistical deviation in assignment volume across agents and time implies that workload distribution is operationally deterministic rather than demand-driven or reactive, eliminating resource pressure as a current risk.

Since total and cumulative assignments are identically distributed, observed monthly fluctuations represent rotational or procedural allocation—not responsiveness to incident volume, urgency, or capacity constraints.
summary: The analysis conclusively shows no evidence of agent overload or increasing assignments—the core business concern is not supported by the data. Workload is perfectly balanced across all five agents (100 assignments each), with no sustained temporal increase for any individual. Monthly variations exist but cancel out over time, confirming a controlled, equitable allocation process rather than an emergent bottleneck or capacity strain. This suggests operational governance is functioning as designed; the next focus should be validating whether this rigid balance aligns with actual handling capacity, priority complexity, or resolution time differences across agents.
